title: Your first test
instruction:
Mark a function with `#[test]` to make it a test. The test passes if it does not panic.

Run this.
successMessage: assert_eq! panics if the two values are not equal. A panicking test is a failing test. Here both assertions pass.

title: assert! macro
instruction:
`assert!` checks that a condition is true.

Run this.
successMessage: assert!(condition) panics when condition is false. assert!(!is_even(3)) verifies 3 is NOT even.

title: assert_ne!
instruction:
`assert_ne!` checks that two values are NOT equal.

Run this.
successMessage: assert_ne! is the 'not equal' version. It fails when the values ARE equal.

title: Custom failure messages
instruction:
All assert macros accept an optional format string for better error messages.

Run this.
successMessage: Custom messages make failures easier to debug. The format string appears in the test output when the assertion fails.

title: #[should_panic]
instruction:
Mark a test with `#[should_panic]` when it should panic to pass.

Run this.
successMessage: expected = means the panic message must contain that string. Without expected, any panic passes. With it, only the matching panic passes.

title: Test organisation in mod tests
instruction:
Convention: put tests in a `mod tests` block with `#[cfg(test)]`.

Run this.
successMessage: #[cfg(test)] means the module is compiled only during tests. use super::* imports everything from the parent module.

title: Result in tests
instruction:
Tests can return `Result<(), E>` — return Err to fail, Ok(()) to pass.

Run this.
successMessage: Returning Result from a test lets you use ? to propagate errors cleanly. No need to unwrap everything in the test body.

title: Test-driven development
instruction:
Write the test first, watch it fail, then implement the function.

Run this.
successMessage: TDD: tests first, then implementation. The tests document what fibonacci should do. The implementation satisfies them.
